A ultra-clean, ultra-wide screenshot of a professional events calendar interface. Light white background, clean modern UI. Shows a monthly calendar grid with coloured event dots and labels on dates — blue dots for North America events, purple for Europe. Left sidebar shows filter options (region, country, category). Top bar has month navigation. Event cards visible on certain dates showing city names like Philadelphia, London, Cannes. Clean blue and purple accent colours, white background, Stripe-quality UI, Apple product screenshot style, 1400x800px, no watermarks, no text outside the UI.

You said:

can we make it look cooler than that with images please

ChatGPT said:

Yeah — that one you made is clean, but it still feels a bit generic SaaS dashboard.

If you want it to feel cool / premium / real product, you need to introduce visual richness without breaking clarity.

The trick is:
👉 keep the clean calendar
👉 add image-backed events + subtle depth

🔥 Upgrade the concept

Instead of:

  • plain calendar grid

  • text labels only

Do:

  • image thumbnails inside event cells

  • hover/expanded event cards

  • soft depth + shadows

  • slight colour glow tied to regions

Think:
👉 Apple Calendar meets Airbnb meets Stripe

💻 USE THIS IMPROVED PROMPT

Create an ultra-clean, premium events calendar interface screenshot for a platform called "Investigator Events".

Layout:
- Ultra-wide (1400x800px)
- Light white / cool off-white background
- Clean modern UI with subtle depth and soft shadows

Main calendar:
- Monthly calendar grid (May 2026)
- Each date cell is clean and minimal
- Some dates contain event cards, not just dots

Event styling:
- Events appear as small rounded cards inside date cells
- Each event card includes:
  - a small image thumbnail (city skyline or landmark)
  - event name (short)
  - city + country
- Example locations:
  - Philadelphia, USA
  - London, UK
  - Cannes, France
  - Prague, Czech Republic

Colour system:
- Blue accents for North America
- Purple accents for Europe
- Subtle coloured glow or border on event cards based on region
- No harsh colours, keep it refined and premium

Sidebar (left):
- Filters panel with:
  - Region
  - Country
  - Event type
- Minimal icons, clean spacing

Top bar:
- Month navigation (May 2026)
- Left/right arrows
- "Today" button
- Investigator Events logo top left

Visual polish:
- Soft shadows under cards
- Very subtle gradients (no muddy blends)
- Crisp typography (modern sans-serif)
- High-end product feel (Stripe / Apple style)

Extra polish:
- One or two event cards slightly elevated to feel interactive
- Subtle depth layering between calendar and cards

Style:
- ultra modern
- clean
- premium
- minimal but visually rich
- not generic SaaS

NO:
- clutter
- heavy gradients
- dark mode
- fake glassmorphism
- excessive glow

Output:
- realistic product screenshot
- no text outside UI
- no watermark
- high resolution

🧠 Why this is better

This version:

  • still shows functionality clearly

  • but now feels like a real product people want to use

  • adds:

    • context (images)

    • hierarchy (cards vs empty days)

    • personality (locations feel real)

⚡ If you want it EVEN better

Next level after this:

You said:

can you give me the link to some really impressive futuristic like awwwards level mobile sits which i can pinch ideas off for my website

ChatGPT said:

Good move — this is exactly how you level up fast. Don’t guess design… steal patterns from the best.

Here are proper Awwwards-level / product-quality mobile sites you should study (not random Dribbble fluff):

🔥 BEST SITES TO STUDY (REAL, HIGH LEVEL)

1. Stripe (gold standard)

👉 https://stripe.com

Why:

  • insane layout discipline

  • perfect spacing

  • mobile feels like a product, not a website

  • subtle motion done right

Steal:

  • section rhythm

  • typography hierarchy

  • calm gradients

2. Linear (VERY relevant to you)

👉 https://linear.app

Why:

  • feels like a real tool

  • clean, sharp, minimal

  • no fluff

Steal:

  • how they present features as product

  • tight mobile spacing

  • card system

3. Raycast

👉 https://raycast.com

Why:

  • modern, crisp, dev/product vibe

  • great mobile flow

Steal:

  • how they structure sections

  • compact hero

  • smooth transitions

4. Vercel

👉 https://vercel.com

Why:

  • clean + technical + premium

  • very controlled design

Steal:

  • minimalism without being boring

  • layout grid discipline

5. Mapbox (you already found this — good instinct)

👉 https://mapbox.com

Why:

  • strong visual storytelling

  • great mobile breakdown

Steal:

  • how visuals support content (not dominate it)

6. Resend

👉 https://resend.com

Why:

  • ridiculously clean

  • perfect mobile density

Steal:

  • spacing

  • typography

  • simplicity

7. Notion (especially mobile)

👉 https://notion.so

Why:

  • product-first thinking

  • very clear hierarchy

Steal:

  • how they explain usefulness quickly

8. Pitch

👉 https://pitch.com

Why:

  • strong visuals but still controlled

Steal:

  • how they mix product + visuals

9. Framer

👉 https://framer.com

Why:

  • modern web polish

  • good mobile responsiveness

Steal:

  • animation restraint (when done right)

10. Awwwards collections (browse properly)

👉 https://www.awwwards.com/websites/mobile/

👉 https://www.awwwards.com/websites/clean/

🧠 WHAT TO ACTUALLY LOOK FOR (IMPORTANT)

Don’t just scroll and go “that looks cool”.

Look for:

1. Section rhythm

  • how tall each section is

  • spacing consistency

  • how content flows

2. Hero discipline

  • small, sharp, not overloaded

  • clear CTA immediately

3. Card systems

  • consistent padding

  • consistent radius

  • consistent density

4. Mobile density

  • nothing feels oversized

  • everything is easy to scan

5. Visual restraint

  • not everything is trying to be cool

  • only 1–2 standout moments

⚠️ WHAT TO AVOID (CRITICAL)

Do NOT copy:

  • crazy animations

  • 3D gimmicks

  • scroll hijacking

  • overdesigned hero sections

That’s how you break your site again.

🎯 FOR YOUR SITE SPECIFICALLY

You should aim for:

👉 Linear + Stripe hybrid
NOT:
👉 Awwwards experimental landing page

Because your product is:

  • practical

  • industry-facing

  • needs trust

If you want next step

I can:

  • take your current homepage

  • and give you exact changes to match Stripe-level quality

Not theory — actual fixes.

Just say 👍

You said:

ChatGPT said:

Got it — using your naming:

🌓 Dark vs Light

✅ Light version (second image) = the one to use

This should be your main logo.

Why:

  • cleaner and more modern

  • reads instantly as “IE”

  • fits your white / blue / pink site perfectly

  • works across UI (navbar, buttons, mobile, favicon)

⚠️ Dark version (first image)

Use only as a secondary / optional variant.

Why not main:

  • more dated feel

  • too “designed”

  • less flexible in UI

🧠 Best setup (important)

You should have:

1. Primary logo

👉 Light version

  • default everywhere

  • homepage

  • navbar

  • marketing

2. Inverted version (for dark backgrounds)

👉 same logo but:

  • white or light version

  • simplified (no heavy gradients ideally)

3. Flat version (very important)

👉 single colour (black or white)

Use for:

  • small sizes

  • favicons

  • emails

  • overlays

🚀 Final decision

👉 Light = main brand
👉 Dark = optional variant (rare use)

If you want, I can:

  • and give you a full export pack (SVG, PNG, favicon, app icon, etc)


You said:

Do u think its perfect as is or is there anything more we should do to it

ChatGPT said:

It’s very good — like genuinely strong — but it’s not quite “final logo system” level yet.

Right now it’s:
👉 great concept + 85–90% execution

To make it feel like a proper product brand (Stripe / Linear level), there are a few small but important refinements.

🔍 What’s already working

  • IE integration into the globe → ✅ strong idea

  • circular shape → ✅ scalable, versatile

  • flow of the “e” → ✅ clean + modern

  • colour direction → ✅ fits your site

You’re not far off at all.

⚠️ What’s holding it back (subtle but important)

1. Gradient is slightly too “designy”

Right now:

  • a bit too vibrant

  • slightly early-2000s tech feel

👉 Fix:

  • tone it down

  • make it more muted + premium

  • think: Stripe blue, not gaming blue

2. Stroke thickness inconsistency

Some parts:

  • outer ring = thicker

  • inner IE shapes = thinner

👉 makes it feel slightly unbalanced

👉 Fix:

  • unify stroke weight or make contrast more intentional

3. The “i” dot placement

It works, but:

  • feels slightly floating / detached

👉 Fix:

  • align it more deliberately with the curve

  • or reduce its size slightly

4. Too much depth illusion

Even in the “light” version:

  • still hints of 3D / bevel

👉 modern logos are:

  • flatter

  • sharper

  • more geometric

👉 Fix:

  • reduce shading

  • keep it mostly 2D with minimal depth

5. Edge sharpness

Some curves feel:

  • slightly soft / anti-aliased

👉 Fix:

  • ensure vector-perfect edges

  • crisp at all sizes

🧠 The real test

Ask:

Would this still look elite at 16px?

If not → needs simplification

✅ What “final” should feel like

When it’s done, it should:

  • work in black and white

  • work at favicon size

  • feel good on:

    • navbar

    • app icon

    • loading screen

  • not rely on gradient to look good

🚀 My honest verdict

👉 You should refine it slightly before locking it in

NOT:
❌ redesign
NOT:
❌ start again

Just:
✅ clean + tighten + simplify

⚡ If you want next step

I can give you:

Option A

👉 exact Figma instructions to fix it in 10–15 mins

Option B

👉 a final refined version spec you can recreate perfectly

Option C

👉 full logo system pack plan (SVG, favicon, dark mode, etc)

Just tell me which 👍


You said:

Okay go can you do the asjustments now do B

ChatGPT said:

Perfect — this is exactly the right move. We’re not redesigning, we’re tightening it into a proper product-grade logo.

Here’s your final refined spec (Option B) — follow this and you’ll end up with something that actually feels finished, not just “good”.

🎯 FINAL REFINED LOGO SPEC (INVESTIGATOR EVENTS)

1. Overall Shape (keep this)

  • Perfect circle base

  • No distortion

  • No perspective / tilt

  • Flat 2D geometry

🎨 2. Colour & Gradient (IMPORTANT FIX)

Current issue:

Too vibrant / slightly dated

Fix:

Use this gradient:

  • Start (left/top): #1E3A8A (deep blue)

  • Mid: #2563EB (clean product blue)

  • End (right/bottom): #22D3EE (soft cyan)

👉 Apply as:

  • linear gradient

  • angle: 135°

Rules:

  • NO harsh contrast

  • NO neon glow

  • smooth transition only

✏️ 3. Stroke & Shape Consistency

Fix thickness:

Make all main elements feel balanced:

  • Outer ring thickness: 100%

  • Inner “e” stroke: ~85–90% of outer

  • Inner “i” stem: match “e” thickness

👉 Right now yours is slightly inconsistent — this cleans it instantly

🔵 4. The “i” Dot (small but important)

Adjust:

  • reduce size by ~10–15%

  • move slightly closer to stem

  • align with curve flow (not floating)

👉 It should feel anchored, not decorative

🧊 5. Remove Fake 3D

Kill:

  • inner bevel shading

  • metallic highlights

  • fake reflections

Keep:

  • flat colour

  • maybe very subtle inner contrast, but barely noticeable

👉 Think:
Stripe / Linear / Notion
NOT:
old tech logos

✨ 6. Edge Quality (CRITICAL)

  • All curves must be vector perfect

  • No blur softness

  • No fuzzy anti-alias edges

👉 Zoom in — fix anchor points if needed

⚖️ 7. Visual Balance

Check this:

  • “e” should not feel heavier than left side

  • ring should not overpower inner letters

  • spacing between shapes = even + intentional

👉 Aim for calm symmetry, not chaos

⚫ 8. Flat Version (YOU NEED THIS)

Create:

Version A (primary)

→ gradient version

Version B (flat)

→ solid colour:

  • Black: #0B1220

  • White: #FFFFFF

👉 If it doesn’t look good in flat → it’s not finished

📏 9. Export Set (FINAL PACK)

Make these:

Core

  • SVG (master)

  • PNG 1024px

  • PNG 512px

  • PNG 256px

UI

  • favicon (32px, 16px)

  • app icon (rounded safe zone)

Variants

  • gradient (main)

  • black

  • white

🧠 FINAL TEST

Before calling it done:

Ask:

✔ Does it still look elite at 24px?

✔ Does it work in black only?

✔ Would a serious SaaS use this?

✔ Does it feel calm, not flashy?

If yes → you’re done.

🚀 REAL TALK

You’re VERY close.

This is no longer about “designing a logo”
This is about:

👉 removing the last 10% of amateur signals

Do these tweaks and it goes from:
“nice logo” → “real product brand”

If you want next:
I can help you:

  • apply this exactly in Figma step-by-step

  • or check your final version and give a yes/no before you lock it in 👍

🔍 Honest breakdown

✅ What this version does better

1. Feels more “brandable”

  • the slight pink adds identity

  • separates you from generic SaaS blue logos

👉 without it, you’re drifting into:

“random fintech startup”

2. More modern (when subtle)

  • gives it a bit of personality without killing professionalism

  • makes the shape feel more dynamic

Use:

  • #EC4899 (muted pink)

  • blend it smoother into the blue

👉 NOT neon / not glowing

2. Integrate it into gradient (not separate)

Right now:
👉 it looks like a patch

Fix:
👉 make it part of the main gradient flow

So instead of:

  • blue → cyan

  • random pink

Do:

  • deep blue → blue → slight pink blend → cyan

3. Reduce coverage

Pink should be:
👉 5–10% accent max

Not a full section

🧠 Strategic advice (important)

You’re building:
👉 a global investigator platform

So the logo needs to feel:

  • serious enough for professionals

  • modern enough to grow into a product

🚀 Final call

BEST VERSION =

👉 this version
✅ WITH toned-down pink
✅ properly blended
✅ not dominant

⚡ If you want the perfect finish

I can:

  • generate the final polished version

  • with:

    • correct gradient

    • perfect balance

    • production-ready feel

Just say:
👉 “final version”


You said:

Okay go do it now

ChatGPT said:


You said:

Okay now make a tech pack

ChatGPT said:

Good — this is exactly the step most people skip. This is what turns a “nice logo” into an actual usable brand system.

Here’s your full tech pack — clean, practical, no fluff.

🎯 INVESTIGATOR EVENTS — LOGO TECH PACK

1. MASTER LOGO (PRIMARY)

Name:

ie-logo-primary-gradient

Description:

  • Circular IE globe mark

  • Blue → cyan gradient with subtle pink accent

  • Used on light backgrounds

Usage:

  • Website navbar

  • Homepage

  • Marketing assets

  • Social

2. COLOUR SPEC (FINAL)

Primary Gradient

#1E3A8A  →  #2563EB  →  #22D3EE

Accent Pink (SUBTLE ONLY)

#EC4899 (5–10% usage max)

Neutrals

#0B1220 (primary text / dark)
#FFFFFF (light)
#F8FAFC (background)

3. LOGO VARIANTS (YOU NEED ALL)

A. Gradient (Main)

  • full colour version

  • use on light backgrounds

B. Dark Version

  • white logo on dark background

  • simplified gradient or flat white

C. Flat Black

  • single colour #0B1220

  • for:

    • email

    • print

    • overlays

D. Flat White

  • pure white version

  • for dark UI / images

E. Icon Only

  • just the circular IE mark

  • no text ever

4. EXPORT FILES

MUST CREATE:

Core

  • logo.svg (MASTER — most important)

  • logo.png (1024px)

  • logo@512.png

  • logo@256.png

UI / Web

  • favicon-32.png

  • favicon-16.png

  • apple-touch-icon.png (180px)

Social

  • logo-square-1024.png

  • logo-twitter.png

  • logo-linkedin.png

5. SPACING RULE (IMPORTANT)

Clear space:

Use “i dot” height as unit

Minimum padding around logo = 1x dot height

Never:

  • crowd it

  • place near edges

  • overlay text on it

6. MINIMUM SIZE

Digital:

  • min: 24px

Ideal:

  • 32px+

  • 48px+ for clarity

7. DO NOTS (STRICT)

❌ Don’t:

  • stretch

  • rotate

  • change colours randomly

  • add glow

  • add shadows

  • put on busy backgrounds without contrast

8. BACKGROUND RULES

Light background:

→ use gradient logo

Dark background:

→ use white version

Image background:

→ use:

  • white version OR

  • add subtle backdrop blur

9. PRODUCT USAGE (YOUR SITE)

Navbar

  • small icon only

  • no text needed

Hero

  • can be subtle / watermark

App feel

  • treat it like an app icon, not a banner logo

10. BRAND POSITIONING (IMPORTANT)

This logo should feel like:

  • global

  • structured

  • modern

  • calm

  • professional

NOT:

  • playful

  • startup-y

  • gimmicky

  • overdesigned

🚀 FINAL STEP (DO THIS NOW)

Put files here:

/public/brand/

Example:

/public/brand/logo.svg
/public/brand/logo-white.svg
/public/brand/favicon-32.png
/public/brand/apple-touch-icon.png

⚡ If you want next level

I can:

  • set up your Next.js favicon + metadata properly

  • or design your full brand system (buttons, colours, UI rules)

That’s what will actually make the site feel like a real product 👍


You said:

How do i do that bruh

ChatGPT said:

Do it the fast way.

1. Make a folder

Inside your project:

Bash
mkdir -p public/brand

2. Save your final logo image into it

Put your chosen logo in there as:

Bash
public/brand/logo-primary.png

If you have the white version too:

Bash
public/brand/logo-white.png

If you only have one PNG right now, that’s fine. Start with that.

3. Make the basic sizes fast

Use a free resizer like Photopea or any image resize site.

Create these from the same logo:

Bash
public/brand/logo-1024.png
public/brand/logo-512.png
public/brand/logo-256.png
public/brand/favicon-32.png
public/brand/favicon-16.png
public/brand/apple-touch-icon.png

Best sizes:

  • logo-1024.png → 1024x1024

  • logo-512.png → 512x512

  • logo-256.png → 256x256

  • favicon-32.png → 32x32

  • favicon-16.png → 16x16

  • apple-touch-icon.png → 180x180

If you want the quickest possible route, just duplicate the same square logo and resize it.

4. Use it in your navbar

If you’re on Next.js, do this:

TypeScript
import Image from "next/image";

<Image
  src="/brand/logo-256.png"
  alt="Investigator Events"
  width={32}
  height={32}
  className="h-8 w-8"
/>

5. Set favicon properly

In your app/layout.tsx metadata, add:

TypeScript
export const metadata = {
  title: "Investigator Events",
  icons: {
    icon: "/brand/favicon-32.png",
    shortcut: "/brand/favicon-32.png",
    apple: "/brand/apple-touch-icon.png",
  },
};

If your setup is slightly different, the main thing is those files need to live in public/brand.

6. If you want the white version

Open your logo in Photopea:

  • Image → Adjustments → make background transparent if needed

  • recolour the logo white

  • export as PNG

Save as:

Bash
public/brand/logo-white.png

Use that on dark backgrounds.

7. Minimum usable setup

If you’re rushed, just do these 4 files:

Bash
public/brand/logo-256.png
public/brand/logo-white.png
public/brand/favicon-32.png
public/brand/apple-touch-icon.png

That is enough to get moving.

8. Easiest actual workflow

If you’re sat there thinking “this is still long”, do this:

  1. Open your final logo image in Preview or Photopea

  2. Export one square PNG at 1024x1024

  3. Resize copies to 256, 32, 16, 180

  4. Drop them into public/brand

  5. Use /brand/logo-256.png in the navbar

  6. Use /brand/favicon-32.png in metadata

Done.

If you want, send me your current app/layout.tsx and navbar component and I’ll tell you the exact lines to paste.


You said:

Wait can u give me prompt for claude code for the colours uve used in this logo please

ChatGPT said:

Yeah this is a good move — lock your colours into the system so everything matches the logo.

Here’s a clean Claude Code prompt you can paste straight in:

🎯 CLAUDE CODE PROMPT (COLOUR SYSTEM)

Read my Tailwind config and global styles.

I want to implement a consistent colour system based on my brand logo.

Use the following colours:

Primary gradient:
- #1E3A8A (deep blue)
- #2563EB (clean blue)
- #22D3EE (cyan)

Accent colour (very subtle use only):
- #EC4899 (pink accent, max 5–10% usage)

Neutrals:
- #0B1220 (primary dark text)
- #FFFFFF (white)
- #F8FAFC (light background)

Tasks:

1. Add these colours to my Tailwind config under a clean naming system:
   - primary (with shades if needed)
   - accent
   - neutral

2. Create a reusable gradient utility for:
   "blue → cyan" using the primary colours

3. Add a secondary gradient that optionally includes the pink accent but keep it subtle and not default.

4. Update global styles so:
   - background uses a clean light tone (#F8FAFC or white)
   - text uses #0B1220
   - no random colours remain in the codebase

5. Replace any existing inconsistent blues, purples, or gradients in the UI with this new system.

6. Ensure:
   - colours are used consistently across buttons, cards, badges, and links
   - gradients are not overused
   - accent pink is only used for small highlights (not large surfaces)

Do not change layout or structure.
Only update colour usage and design tokens.

Output only the updated Tailwind config and any modified global styles.

🧠 Why this works

This will:

  • stop your UI looking random

  • align everything with your logo

  • instantly make it feel more “product-level”

⚠️ One important thing

If Claude starts:

  • adding loads of gradients everywhere

  • making everything pink/blue

👉 stop it

You want:

  • 90% clean UI

  • 10% colour

If you want next:
I can give you:
👉 exact button / card styles using these colours so your UI stops looking scuffed 👍


You said:

For this logo
2/2

ChatGPT said:

Yep — for this exact logo, use this Claude prompt:

Read my Tailwind config and global styles.

I want to build a colour system based exactly on this logo.

Base the system on these brand colours:

Core blues/cyan:
- #0F1F9A
- #1F56F0
- #35CFEF

Pink accent:
- #F15AD9

Supporting dark:
- #0B1220

Light neutrals:
- #FFFFFF
- #F8FAFC
- #EEF4FF

Tasks:

1. Add these colours to my Tailwind config using a clean naming system:
   - brand.navy
   - brand.blue
   - brand.cyan
   - brand.pink
   - brand.ink
   - brand.white
   - brand.surface
   - brand.surfaceAlt

2. Create reusable gradient tokens/utilities based on the logo:
   - primary gradient: navy → blue → cyan
   - accent gradient: blue → pink (subtle use only)

3. Update global styles so:
   - default page backgrounds use white / very light cool surface tones
   - main text uses brand.ink
   - cards use clean cool-white surfaces
   - borders use soft cool tones derived from the palette

4. Replace any inconsistent blues, purples, cyans, or random gradients in the UI with this new brand system.

5. Add guidance in code comments or naming so:
   - pink is only used sparingly for accents/highlights
   - the main UI remains mostly blue/cyan with strong neutral balance
   - gradients are used on key CTAs, highlights, and selected states only
   - large surfaces should not become overly pink or overly saturated

6. Make sure the resulting palette feels:
   - premium
   - modern
   - clean
   - product-focused
   - suitable for a white/light UI

Do NOT change layout or structure.
Do NOT redesign components.
Only update design tokens, colour usage, and global styling.

Output only:
1. updated Tailwind config
2. updated global styles
3. any small colour token replacements needed

If you want, I can also give you the actual hex picks mapped from the logo into button / card / border / hover states so Claude doesn’t improvise.


You said:

No u make one of theze i meant for it make it light themed for sure though

ChatGPT said:


You said:

Make me a first linkedin post graphic for the company page what should we make heres the about us page INVESTIGATOR EVENTS GLOBAL EVENT DISCOVERY Events Associations Forum Submit Event Advice About Why Join? ￼ ABOUT What Investigator Events is, and why it exists. A practical calendar for the private investigations sector. It brings live conferences, training, and association events into one place so professionals can compare timing, check provenance, and see who is behind each listing. ONE PLACE TO BROWSE Track investigator conferences, training, and association meetings worldwide in one clear calendar. A CLEARER PLANNING VIEW Compare timing more quickly and reduce the risk of avoidable date clashes. CLEAR LINKS BEHIND EACH EVENT See which association or organiser is behind a listing before you commit time or travel. WHY TRUST IT Built by someone with real standing in the profession. Mike LaCorte Founder, Investigator Events I help law firms, corporates, and HNWIs solve complex cross-border investigations. 25+ years in international investigations Spear's 500 Top Investigator Investigator of the Year (2016) President, Association of British Investigators Former President, World Association of Detectives Global investigations leadership International Investigations Asset Tracing Corporate Risk Litigation Support OSINT / HUMINT Investigator Events was built because there was no neutral, genuinely useful global calendar for this space. It exists to make legitimate events easier to find, compare, and trust at a glance. FROM THE FOUNDER Why this exists I have spent my career in international investigations and I still feel lucky to be part of this world. It is a community before it is an industry. The people in it are sharp, generous, fiercely independent, and surprisingly good company at a hotel bar after a long day of conference sessions. Over the years I have been fortunate enough to hold senior positions across several of the associations that serve this profession, as President of the Association of British Investigators, Secretary General of the IKD, and through President and Chairman roles within the World Association of Detectives. Those roles gave me a front-row seat to an industry with great events, run by good people, that many investigators never heard about until it was too late. The problem was never a shortage of conferences, training, or association gatherings. The problem was that nobody could see the full picture. Every organisation promoted its own calendar, on its own website, to its own members. Dates clashed without anyone realising. Investigators booked one event and missed another they would have loved. Organisers set dates in the dark. I care about this because I have seen what happens when people actually show up. Conferences are where investigators meet the person they will trust with a referral three years later. They are where someone early in their career realises how far this profession can take them. They are where association leaders sit together and quietly move things forward for everyone else. When those gatherings are easy to find and well attended, the whole community benefits. I also care about the associations themselves. The people who run them do it because they believe in the profession, often voluntarily, often without much thanks. If a shared calendar means better attendance at their events and fewer avoidable clashes, that alone makes this worth doing. Investigator Events is a free platform, built for a community I am proud to be part of. It is not a commercial layer between associations and their members. It is a single calendar that makes the industry easier to see, easier to plan around, and a little harder to miss. Mike LaCorte Founder, Investigator Events WHAT YOU CAN EXPECT A cleaner, more useful way to navigate the event calendar. The platform is designed to help professionals assess relevance quickly, understand provenance, and make decisions with less friction. 1 Live event listings with clear dates, locations, and organiser details. 2 Clear links between events and the association or organiser behind them. 3 Free submissions, with review before publication so the calendar stays credible. NEXT STEP Browse the network or go straight to the live calendar. Browse eventsView associations INVESTIGATOR EVENTS Every investigator event. One global calendar. Browse live events, follow associations, and list conferences, training, and meetings for free. EXPLORE • Calendar • Associations • Weekly • Find a PI • Directory map • Why join an association? • About • Privacy • Terms of Service • Community Guidelines LIST • Submit event • Advertise or partner • Email updates • Advice CONTACT info@investigatorevents.com Global coverage. Free listings. Reviewed submissions. EMAIL UPDATES Read the weekly page or subscribe for new events, approaching dates, and one useful featured listing. (c) 2026 Investigator Events About | Investigator Events

another industry event.

training, and association events.

NOT:

Different associations. Different countries.
Different calendars. No clear view.

→ Compare dates
→ Know who’s behind each event

You said:

How does it work for these though needs to work for all these

ChatGPT said:

Yeah this is where most people mess it up — and you’re right to catch it now.

⚠️ Reality check

LinkedIn banners are not consistent across devices:

  • Desktop → full wide banner ✅

  • Laptop → slightly cropped

  • Mobile → HEAVILY cropped (center only) ❌

👉 Your current banner works on desktop
👉 It will break on mobile (text gets cut)

🎯 SOLUTION: SAFE ZONE DESIGN

You need to design for this:

🟦 SAFE AREA (THIS IS ALL THAT MATTERS)

Only this middle strip is guaranteed visible:

|    ❌ cropped    |   ✅ SAFE ZONE   |   ❌ cropped    |

Actual rule:

👉 Keep ALL important content inside center ~60% width

🔧 WHAT YOU NEED TO CHANGE

❌ Current issue

  • text is too left

  • logo is too right

  • both risk getting cut on mobile

✅ FIXED LAYOUT

CENTER EVERYTHING

Instead of:

[text left]       [logo right]

Do:

        [text]
        [logo]

🧠 PERFECT STRUCTURE (WORKS ON ALL DEVICES)

CENTERED STACK

Top:

The global calendar for
investigator events

Under:

Conferences · Training · Associations

Then:

👉 small logo underneath OR faint behind

🎨 VISUAL CHANGE

Instead of:

  • big right-side logo ❌

Do:

  • faded background globe (centered)
    OR

  • small centered logo under text

📱 WHY THIS WORKS

On mobile:

  • LinkedIn crops sides HARD

  • center survives

So:
👉 centered design = always safe
👉 side layout = breaks

🔥 QUICK FIX (DO THIS NOW)

Open your banner in Figma / Canva:

  1. Draw a rectangle in center (~900px width)

  2. Move ALL text inside it

  3. Move logo inside it

  4. Reduce logo size slightly

  5. Export again

🚀 FINAL RESULT

After this:

  • desktop = clean

  • mobile = still readable

  • looks intentional everywhere

👇 If you want

I can:
👉 regenerate a perfectly safe-zone optimized version
(specifically built for LinkedIn cropping)

Just say:
“fix for mobile”

Your design is:

  • too tall visually (waves + map stacked)

  • too much happening vertically

But LinkedIn is forcing:
👉 super thin horizontal band

So it looks:
❌ cramped
❌ cut off
❌ like it doesn’t belong

Ultra-horizontal composition

Think like this:

-----------------------------------------
   (everything important happens HERE)
-----------------------------------------

NOT:

big waves
map
gradient
layers

1. Kill vertical complexity

Remove:

  • big bottom wave ❌

  • top gradient blob ❌

Keep:
👉 ONE clean horizontal flow

2. Make it “strip-based”

New composition:

  • very light background

  • faint dotted world map (centered)

  • thin flowing gradient line (left → right)

  • small glowing nodes across map

That’s it.

3. No heavy elements

👉 Your current issue:

  • bottom wave is too dominant

Replace with:

  • subtle thin gradient ribbon

🧠 Think like this

Not:

“cool hero section”

But:

“Apple header background texture”

🔥 What yours should look like

Visually:

  • white / light grey base

  • super faint world map

  • small glowing dots (events)

  • one elegant curved line crossing horizontally

  • tiny pink accent on left (VERY subtle)

⚡ Why this works

  • nothing important gets cropped

  • looks clean on ALL devices

  • feels premium, not forced

👇 Real advice (important)

You were right earlier:

“we just need a cool graphic”

That’s EXACTLY it.

No:

  • text

  • logos

  • messaging

and I’ll fix it properly — not just another guess 👍

  • World map is faint + centered → always visible

  • Colours match your brand → blue / cyan + subtle pink
